coredata.dc:title: Codoping g-C3N4 with boron and graphene quantum dots: Enhancement of charge transfer for ultrasensitive and selective photoelectrochemical detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Longhui
Zhang, Haobo
Won, Miae
Kim, Eunji
Li, Mingle
Kim, Jong Seung
coredata.dc:description: The development of superior photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors for biosensing has become a major objective of PEC research. However, conventional PEC-active materials are typically constrained by a weak photocurrent response owing to their limited surface-active sites and high electron-hole recombination rate. Here, a boron and graphene quantum dots codoped g-C3N4 (named GBCN) as PEC sensor for highly sensitive dopamine (DA) detection was fabricated. GBCN exhibited the greatest photocurrent response and PEC activity compared to free g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 doped with boron. The proposed PEC sensor for DA determination exhibited a broad linear range (0.001–800 μM) and a low detection limit (0.96 nM). In particular, a sensitivity up to 10.3771 μA/μM/cm2 was seen in the case of GBCN. The high PEC activity can be attributed to the following factors: (1) the boron and graphene quantum dots co-doping significantly increased the specific surface area of g-C3N4, providing more adsorption sites for DA; (2) the dopants extended the absorption intensity of g-C3N4, red-shifting the absorption from 470 to 540 nm; and (3) the synergism of boron and graphene quantum dots efficiently boosted the photogenerated electrons migration from the conduction band of g-C3N4 to graphene quantum dots, facilitating charge separation. In addition, GBCN also exhibited good anti-interference ability and stability. This research may shed light on the creation of a highly sensitive and selective PEC platform for detecting biomolecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical sensor
Dopamine
g-C3N4
Co-doping

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The mechanism of action of GBCN for DA detection
Analytical performance of the PEC nanosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of (A) CN, (B) BCN, and (C) GBCN. (D) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy and element mapping of GBCN. (E) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CN, BCN, and GBCN. High-resolution XPS spectrums of (F) C 1s, (G) N 1s, (H) B 1s and (I) S 2p of GBCN.

(A) Photocurrent of CN, BCN and GBCN. (B) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of CN, BCN and GBCN. Insets was the pore-size distributions of samples. (C) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of CN, BCN and GBCN.
(A) Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, (B) Mott–Schottky, and (C) EIS plots for CN, BCN and GBCN.

(A) Photocurrent of GBCN in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.015, 0.02, 0.025, 0.03, 0.04, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.32, 0.37, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700 and 800 μM DA (from left to right), and (B) corresponding calibration curves for different concentrations of DA. (C) Specificity of the proposed PEC sensing toward DA at 10 μM, NE and 5-HT at 20 μM, Tyr, UA, AA, Cys and glu at 50 μM. (D) Stability of the as-prepared GBCN with 0.4 μM DA under 20 “on-off” illumination cycles.
Schematic illustration of the co-doped strategy (i.e., boron and graphene quantum dots) to construct g-C3N4 based nanobiosensor (GBCN) for the detection of dopamine.
Schematic illustration of the charge transfer process of GBCN within the PEC sensing system.
Determination of DA in blood and cerebrospinal fluid samples.

head.ce:title: Codoping g-C3N4 with boron and graphene quantum dots: Enhancement of charge transfer for ultrasensitive and selective photoelectrochemical detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Forestry, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou, 450002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Forestry, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou, 450002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Korea University, Seoul, 02841, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Korea University, Seoul, 02841, Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Forestry, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou, 450002, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of superior photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors for biosensing has become a major objective of PEC research. However, conventional PEC-active materials are typically constrained by a weak photocurrent response owing to their limited surface-active sites and high electron-hole recombination rate. Here, a boron and graphene quantum dots codoped g-C3N4 (named GBCN) as PEC sensor for highly sensitive dopamine (DA) detection was fabricated. GBCN exhibited the greatest photocurrent response and PEC activity compared to free g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 doped with boron. The proposed PEC sensor for DA determination exhibited a broad linear range (0.001–800 μM) and a low detection limit (0.96 nM). In particular, a sensitivity up to 10.3771 μA/μM/cm2 was seen in the case of GBCN. The high PEC activity can be attributed to the following factors: (1) the boron and graphene quantum dots co-doping significantly increased the specific surface area of g-C3N4, providing more adsorption sites for DA; (2) the dopants extended the absorption intensity of g-C3N4, red-shifting the absorption from 470 to 540 nm; and (3) the synergism of boron and graphene quantum dots efficiently boosted the photogenerated electrons migration from the conduction band of g-C3N4 to graphene quantum dots, facilitating charge separation. In addition, GBCN also exhibited good anti-interference ability and stability. This research may shed light on the creation of a highly sensitive and selective PEC platform for detecting biomolecules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a crucial neurotransmitter with multiple important functions, including human metabolism, immune, memory, sleep, and cognition (Berke 2018; Corkrum et al., 2020; Grace 2016; Mohebi et al., 2019; Westbrook et al., 2020). The DA concentration changes are strongly linked to severe physical and neurological disorders. A DA deficiency in the brain may lead to mental disorders like Parkinson’s disease and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, whereases an excess of DA can result in pleasurable, exciting, and sometimes even euphoria feelings (Faraone 2018; Jean-Martin Beaulieu1, 2014; Leggio et al., 2019). The diagnosis of these diseases requires the measurement of the DA concentration in biological samples with a high degree of precision. Several analytical techniques such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, electrochemiluminescence, enzyme analysis, capillary electrophoresis, and fluorescence sensor have been developed to detect DA (Devos et al., 2014; Kato and Kuwata 2021; Zhang et al., 2003). However, the technical complexity, instrument size, lack of sensitivity, and inferior selectivity limit the practical application of these techniques. Consequently, the development of highly sensitive and selective DA detection methods in vivo and in vitro is crucial for clinical diagnosis, monitoring of therapeutic effects, and disease prevention.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing as a dependable electroanalytical technique is gaining increasing interest due to its user-friendly operation, low background signal, and high reproducibility (Ai et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2018; Velmurugan and Yang, 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2022; Zou et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2021, 2022; Sagara et al., 2016). Based on photoactive materials, optical signals can be converted into electrical signals in PEC sensors. When the photon energy absorbed by PEC materials higher than or equal to their energy band, the materials are excited to generate photo-generated carriers (i.e., electron-hole (e– – h+) pairs), which can migrate rapidly from the bulk phase to surface. They can be efficiently utilized only if photogenerated carriers reach the surface and enter the conductive substrates or electrolyte (Sun et al., 2022). In principle, electrons are the primary charge carriers, the greater the number of electrons, the higher the photocurrent response, and consequently the detection sensitivity. Therefore, the key to developing PEC sensors with superior analytical performance lies in the design and preparation of semiconductor materials which has high carrier separation and transport efficiency.

Graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a polymer with two-dimensional layered structure. Its unique surface, size, and quantum effects significantly enhance the detection performance of electrochemical sensors (Chen et al., 2021; Jiménez-Calvo et al., 2019). g-C3N4 is also thermal stabile, chemical inert, and biocompatible, making it as an ideal material for constructing stable and highly sensitive biosensing interfaces (Yan et al., 2020). Nonetheless, their insufficiently exposed active sites and rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons restrict their practical application (Wen et al., 2017). Several modification strategies, such as doping g-C3N4 with non-metal elements like S, P, etc., have been developed to increase the surface area and facilitate the charge migration of nanostructured g-C3N4 (Zhang et al., 2020). Surface loading of quantum dots nanoparticles can offer more active and adsorption sites and facilitate the separation of photogenerated e– – h+ pairs (Li et al., 2019). This indicates that codoping with non-metallic elements and quantum dots nanoparticles are promising methods for modifying g-C3N4 for highly efficient PEC sensing performance.

Boron dopant, as a non-metal element, can generate a large number of unsaturated sites and induce strong interlayer C–N interactions, resulting in potent electron excitation and accelerated charge transport (Zhao et al., 2019). Graphene quantum dots, a family of semiconductor nanocrystals ranging in size from 1–10 nm, are gaining increasing interest in PEC-active material design (Sun et al., 2013; Yan et al., 2019; Ye et al., 2013). Graphene quantum dots have tunable absorption properties, making them promising for extending the nanostructures’ absorption bands (Nie et al., 2018). In addition, graphene quantum dots can function as electron acceptor to promote charge transfer and decrease electron-hole recombination (Liu et al., 2017). This paper explores methods for fabricating a highly active PEC system for DA detection with high sensitivity based on the co-doping of boron and graphene quantum dots (Scheme 1 ). Boron and graphene quantum dots co-doped g-C3N4 possesses a large specific surface, rapid charge transfer capability, and superior photoelectric activity. The introduction of boron and graphene quantum dots increased the photocurrent value of constructed PEC sensor boron and graphene quantum dots co-doped g-C3N4 by a favor of four compared to that of free g-C3N4. In addition, the photocurrent signal gradually increased as DA concentration rose. This work not only sheds light on how to increase the number of exposed active sites and enhance charge transfer but expands the application of a PEC sensor with high stability and selectivity for DA detection.

Melamine, boron oxide, thiourea, dopamine, sodium dihydrogenorthophosphate, sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate, norepinephrine (NE), 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT), Tyramine (Tyr), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys) and glutamic acid (glu) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. NaCl, KCl were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Nafion dispersions (5 wt%) was obtained from DuPont without further purification.

Synthesis of g-C
3
N
4 : 5 g melamine was placed in a ceramic crucible. The ceramic crucible was then transferred to a muffle furnace and calcined at 550 °C for 4 h (2.5 °C/min) in an air atmosphere, finally giving g-C3N4 as a yellow powder.

Synthesis of boron-doped g-C
3
N
4 : 500 mg g-C3N4 was well grounded with B2O3 (2.5 g). The mixture was next calcinated at 360 °C (5 °C/min) in N2 atmosphere for 2 h. When the product was cooled down to room temperature, followed by washing with boiling water and drying at 60 °C, boron-doped g-C3N4 was obtained.

Synthesis of graphene quantum dots: Citric acid (0.63 g) and thiourea (0.69 g) were dissolved in 15 mL water. After stirring for 30 min, the clear solution was moved to a 50 mL stainless autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. Resulting supernatant was desired graphene quantum dots.

Synthesis of boron and graphene quantum dots co-doped g-C
3
N
4 : The boron and graphene quantum dots co-doped g-C3N4 was prepared using an impregnation method. 250 mg boron-doped g-C3N4 was mixed into 3 mL graphene quantum dots solution, then diluted to 10 mL with distilled water. After stirring overnight, the liquid was filtered out by a suction filtration. Obtained filter cake was then dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The composites were boron and graphene quantum dots co-doped g-C3N4.

5 mg boron and graphene quantum dots co-doped g-C3N4 was dispersed into 1 mL absolute ethyl alcohol, then 30 μL suspension was dropped on 1 cm × 1.5 cm indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode with a useable area of 1 cm2 and naturally dried at room temperature. g-C3N4 and boron-doped g-C3N4 electrodes was prepared by the same method. The PEC tests were carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) was used as the electrolyte. A 300 W Xenon arc lamp was used as the light source. DA concentration was detected by the changes of photocurrent.

As noted in the experimental section, the boron and graphene quantum dots co-doping g-C3N4 (named GBCN) was prepared using an impregnation method. To better show that proposed co-doping strategy could enhance the photoelectric activities of g-C3N4, boron-doped g-C3N4 (named BCN) and free g-C3N4 (named CN) were prepared as control. The morphologies of as-designed PEC materials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As can be seen from an inspection of Fig. 1 A and B, CN displayed layered stack structures, and the boron doping did not change its morphologies. After further loading with the graphene quantum dots, small black dots measuring 1–3 nm in diameter were observed on the surface of GBCN (cf. white circles in Fig. 1C), indicating the formation of codoping nanocomposites. High-resolution TEM image revealed that GBCN had a lattice spacing of 0.336 nm (Fig. 1D), a result corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of the composite (Xia et al., 2022). The lattice spacing distance of 0.24 nm in a representative dot was similar to that of graphite (1120) facets (Qu et al., 2013, 2015), suggesting that carbon atoms in graphene quantum dots have sp2 bonded graphitic nature. The element mapping of GBCN was performed to confirm boron and graphene quantum dots codoping.

The crystal structures of CN, BCN, GBCN were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Fig. 1E). All samples exhibited two diffraction peaks at around 13° and 27.5°, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively (Huang et al., 2021). However, a slight shift of peak located at (002) was found after boron doping (cf. BCN and GBCN), which suggested the change in planar strains, tensile stresses, and lattice parameters by boron atoms introducing defects to the g-C3N4 crystalline structure (Chang et al., 2021; Oliveros et al., 2021). Remarkably, no characteristic peak of graphene quantum dots was observed in the XRD patterns of GBCN, which was attributed to its small contents and high dispersion (Che et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of C 1s, N 1s, B 1s and S 2p of GBCN was displayed in Fig. 1F–I. In the C 1s high resolution spectrum, four peaks at 284.87, 286.37, 288.08, and 288.64 eV were seen, corresponding to the graphitic CC, C–Nx, sp2-bonded NC–N and CO from carbonyls and carboxylates, respectively (Yola, 2021). The new peak at 288.64 eV (no such peak was seen for CN and BCN; cf. Fig. S1) in conjunction with the existence of carbonyl and carboxyl groups indicated that the graphene quantum dots were successfully introduced into the surface of the g-C3N4 (Qu et al., 2013). For N 1s spectrum, three obvious peaks at 398.55, 399.32, and 401.07 eV were attributed to the aromatic ring with -C-NC, sp3 bonded N-(C)3 and C–N–H groups (Hu et al., 2021). The XPS spectra of the B 1s at 192.78 eV was originated from the B–N bonds (Fig. 1H) (Kim and Yong, 2021). Additionally, high resolution of S 2p XPS spectrum revealed the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene at 163.28 eV and SO bonding at 168.02 eV (Fig. 1I) (Qu et al., 2013). The sulfur or sulfur oxide dopants in the GBCN structure would induce a higher charge density, facilitating subsequent charge separation (Zhang et al., 2014). Overall, the XPS analysis provided evidence that boron and graphene quantum dots co-doping g-C3N4 was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of action of GBCN for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the superior analytical performance of GBCN toward DA, we then explored its PEC-sensing mechanisms of action (MoA). In general, the mechanism of using photoelectrochemistry for biomolecules detection is based on the so-called photogenerated hole oxidation process (Hun et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2015; Mohammadzadeh kakhki and Yaghoobi Rahni, 2022; Wang et al., 2018a; Yan et al., 2015; Yi et al., 2022). As depicted in Scheme 2 , under conditions of photo-illumination the PEC-active materials (e.g., GBCN) can absorb the photon energy, leading to the electrons transformed from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of the materials, thereby generating the e– – h+ pairs. The holes (h+) left in the VB would oxidize the biomolecules (e.g., DA) that have been adsorbed on the surface of the materials, while the biomolecules can act as the electron donor to release electrons. These electrons travel through the electrodes to form photocurrents, as a result providing corresponding electrochemical signals for analysis.

On this basis, we conclude that a highly sensitive and selective PEC system for DA detection requires three crucial prerequisites: firstly, the PEC-active materials should have a large adsorption active site for DA attachment; secondly, the materials are able to generate more e– – h+ pairs (i.e., they should have the excellent light-absorbing ability); and thirdly, the photoelectron-hole pairs can be effectively separated. In order to elucidate the superior DA-sensing mechanism inherent in GBCN, we therefore investigated its specific surface area, photon absorption capacity, and charge separation efficiency through N2 adsorption-desorption assay, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS), photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, and electrochemical measurement.

The results of the transient photocurrent of CN, BCN and GBCN were shown in Fig. 2 A. In the absence of light, the photocurrent response of each electrode was minimal. Under conditions of light illumination, however, CN exhibited a weak photocurrent of 0.022 μA, indicating that photo-generated e– – h+ pairs of CN could be transferred and separated, and some electrons enter the external circuit via electrode and generate a certain current consequently (Yang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). As inferred from above XRD data, boron doping introduced structural defects into the structure of g-C3N4; this would benefit for the rapid migration and separation of charges (Oliveros et al., 2021). Hence, with boron doping, the photocurrent of BCN electrode was approximately 1.68 times higher than that of CN electrode. Additional doping with graphene quantum endowed GBCN with a better photocurrent value, which was nearly 4 times higher than that of CN. These results provide support for our hypothesis that co-doping of boron atom and graphene quantum dots could improve the photocurrent properties of g-C3N4.

Fig. 2B exhibited the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of CN, BCN, and GBCN. In brief, the specific surface area of BCN and GBCN were 24.47 and 26.56 m2/g, which were 1.58 and 1.72-fold higher than that seen in CN. The large specific surface area was benefited from the variation in pore-size distribution; this would be beneficial for subsequent photon absorption and providing more active sites for redox reactions (Zhu et al., 2022). The photon absorption properties of developed g-C3N4 materials were carefully studied using UV–vis DRS technique (Fig. 2C). As compared to CN, no distinct absorption edge shifting was seen for BCN. However, due to the loading of graphene quantum dots, GBCN exhibited an obvious red shift of absorption edge from ∼470–540 nm, extending to the visible light region; this would facilitate more e− – h+ pairs generation (Wang et al., 2018). The indirect gap energy was obtained by linearly fitting the square root of the absorption energy against the photon energy (Fig. S2). Notably, the band gap energy of GBCN (2.31 eV) was obviously smaller than that of CN or BCN (2.62 eV), indicating the enhanced light-harvesting capability and improved photocatalytic activity of GBCN.

The separation efficiency of photogenerated e– – h+ pairs of GBCN was detected by PL spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the spectral signals of BCN and GBCN were lower than those of CN, indicating that the former significantly inhibits the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Among them, GBCN had the lowest signal intensity, which proved that its separation efficiency of holes and electrons was the highest, indicating more DA biomolecules were oxidized by holes. This result was consistent with Mott-Schottky and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The improved efficiency for electron transfer was evaluated by detecting the conductivity properties of the materials. Fig. 3B displays the Mott–Schottky curves of the three materials. For n–type semiconductors like CN, the relationship between depletion layer capacitance (C
sc ) and applied bias (E) can be described by Eq. (1),
(1)
1 /
C
s c
2
=
2
(
E −
E
F B
−
k T
/ e
)
/
(
ε 0
ε r
N D
A 2
)
wherein e is electron charge; ε
0 is permittivity of vacuum; ε
r is dielectric constant of g-C3N4; A is contact area of electrode in the electrolyte; E
FB is flat–band potential of the semiconductor; and N
D is carrier (electrons for n–type) density. The item
k T
/ e
can be overlooked due to its low value. The E
FB and the N
D in the semiconductor can be obtained according to the slope and intercept of the fitted curves.

According to the fitted equations in Fig. 3B, the E
FB potentials for CN, BCN and GBCN were estimated as −0.87, −0.71 and −0.58 V vs. NHE at pH = 7, and their conduction bands (E
CB ) were −0.97, −0.81 and −0.68 V (E
FB = E
CB – 0.1), respectively. Generally, E
CB corresponds to the position where electrons transfer out. The downward shift of the E
CB after modification by Boron and graphene quantum dots indicated that electrons transferred from conduction band of g-C3N4 to graphene quantum dots, which was beneficial for the separation of photogenerated e− - h+ pairs. Simultaneously, the relative N
D can be estimated according to the slopes of curves. Obviously, GBCN had the highest N
D value. The increased N
D can improve the electron mobility and reduce the electron resistance, which was further supported by EIS results (Fig. 3C). At high frequencies, the charge transfer resistance (R
ct ), which refers to the resistance against electron transfer from the electrode to electrolytes, correlates closely with the diameter of semicircle. According to the results of EIS plots, the GBCN electrode had the lowest R
ct , indicating the electrons transfer through the solid/liquid interface electrode had the least resistance, which would lead to fewer possibilities of charge recombination. Based on the analysis noted above, the mechanism of DA detection was summarized as the more adsorption active sites, stronger light absorption capability, and higher electron-hole separation efficiency owing to boron and graphene quantum dots doping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the PEC nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Motivated by the photocurrent enhancement effect shown in GBCN, the PEC sensing ability of GBCN toward DA was studied. Fig. 4 A reveals the real-time photocurrent signal gradually enhanced with the continuous addition of DA (0.001–800 μM). The initial concentration was 0.001 μM, which was infinitely close to the limit of detection (LOD). The photocurrent gradually enhanced as the DA concentration increased, until the turning point of the linear equation occurred at the concentration of 0.4 μM. At the concentration of 0.4–10 μM, the photocurrent showed a slight increase, then expanding the concentration interval, the linear equation at high DA concentration was obtained. Via the plotting of photocurrent vs DA concentration, two linear calibration ranges of 0.001–0.4 μM and 0.4–800 μM were observed, showing the linear equation of y = 10.3771x + 0.0498 (R2 = 0.9997) and y = 0.0021x + 4.2353 (R2 = 0.9978), respectively (Fig. 4B). Remarkably, the turning point was conformed to both the linearity at low concentration and high concentration. The higher slope at low concentrations of DA was resulted from the greater changes of electron transfer rate than that at high DA concentrations (Kong et al., 2022). LOD of GBCN toward DA was estimated as 0.96 nM, and the detection sensitivity was 10.3771 μA/μM/cm2, much higher than that of previously reported results (Ahmadi et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Of note, in comparison with reported PEC sensors (a summary was shown in Table S1), GBCN presented a lower LOD (ca. 7–500 fold) and wider detection range (ca. 1–80 fold), indicating that GBCN allows for a sensitive and effective detection of DA in vitro.

In addition, considering the facts that interfering compounds possibly coexisted in biological samples, such as NE, 5-HT, Tyr, UA, AA, Cys and glu, the selectivity of GBCN was evaluated toward DA at 10 μM, 5-HT and NE at 20 μM, Tyr, UA, AA, Cys and glu at 50 μM. As shown in Fig. 4C, DA showed obvious PEC response at the concentration of 10 μM. NE and 5-HT also exhibited a certain photocurrent at the concentration of 2 times that of DA, while the PEC response of Tyr, UA, AA, Cys and glu was negligible at even the concentration of 5 times that of DA. According to the detection mechanism, biomolecules attachment was the fundamental reason to cause photocurrent change of the sensor. Therefore, the ζ-potentials of GBCN at pH = 7.4 was tested, the value was as low as −22 mV, which would inhibit adsorption of biomolecules with negative carboxyl group or similar groups on the surface of the catalysts. Hence, negligible photocurrent was detected in AA, Cys and glu systems. DA, NE, 5-HT, Tyr and UA with positive NH2 or similar groups could electrostatically interact with GBCN, and HOMO levels of DA (−4.53 eV), NE (−4.61 eV), 5-HT (−4.67 eV) and Tyr (−6.12 eV) located between the LUMO (−0.50 eV) and HOMO (−8.08 eV) levels of GBCN (Kong et al., 2022; Yadav and Mukherjee, 2017; Qureshi et al., 2022). However, the HOMO energy level DA was closer to the LUMO level of GBCN than those of NE, 5-HT and Tyr, which leaded to its stronger ability to inject electron into the HOMO of GBCN, thus achieving high specificity of DA without recognition biomolecules. Since the HOMO level of UA (−8.85 eV) was beyond the scope of the LUMO and HOMO levels of GBCN (Masoud et al., 2012), the photocurrent of UA was also negligible. Moreover, the concentration of NE and 5-HT was relatively low in cells; the influences of them on DA detection could be ignored. These results demonstrated that the selectivity of the DA photoelectrochemical sensor GBCN was satisfactory.

The stability of the photocurrent was investigated by cycling the light source on/off for 20 cycles. To our delight, no appreciable changes in the photocurrent were seen during repeat operation (Fig. 4D). The relative standard deviation (RSD) value was negligible, indicating the good stability of GBCN. To confirm the storage stability of GBCN, the photocurrent response experiment was carried out at the DA concentration of 0.4 μM by using newly synthesized and 90-day GBCN as working electrode. As shown in Fig. S3, no obvious photocurrent change was found in both systems, indicating excellent stability of GBCN.

The reliability test was carried out to determine the practicability of the GBCN PEC in blood and cerebrospinal fluid. It can be concluded from Table 1 , experimental results obtained were in good agreement with the values of DA in diluted samples. The accuracy for the content determination of DA indicated the excellent recovery of the PEC method. Collectively, these results lead to the conclusion that GBCN may be an ideal PEC sensor for DA detection.

In conclusion, we have developed a new boron/graphene quantum dots co-doping strategy to construct a g–C3N4–based PEC sensor for dopamine detection. The experimental results demonstrated that co-doping g-C3N4 with boron atoms and graphene quantum dots can significantly improve its photocurrent properties. The biosensor GBCN provided a linear PEC response to dopamine with a sensing concentration range of 0.001–800 μM and an ultralow LOD of 0.96 nM (S/N = 3), exhibiting excellent selectivity, adequate linear range, and remarkable sensitivity. The GBCN, which facilitated dopamine adsorption and enhanced light absorption capacity and charge carrier concentrations, is responsible for DA's high sensitivity. This research may provide a novel, efficient method for constructing highly sensitive PEC biosensors for widespread photoelectrochemical applications in early disease diagnosis, environmental monitoring, and food safety assessment.

Longhui Zheng: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing–original draft, Writing–review & editing. Haobo Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources. Miae Won: Writing–review & editing. Eunji Kim: Writing–review & editing. Mingle Li: Conceptualization, Writing–review & editing, Supervision. Jong Seung Kim: Resources, Writing–review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Youth Program of National Natural Science of China (Grant no. 22102051, L.Z.), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (Grant no. 202300410211, L.Z.), the Special Fund for Topnotch Talents in Henan Agricultural University (Grant no. 30500806 and 30500922, L.Z.) and the Science and Technology Innovation Foundation of Henan Agricultural University (Grant no. KJCX2020A07, L.Z.). We also gratefully acknowledge the financial support received from the National Research Foundation of Korea (CRI project no. 2018R1A3B1052702 and 2019M3E5D1A01068998, J.S.K) and the Brain Pool Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (Grant No. 2020H1D3A1A02080172, M.L.)

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
